text: wasn’t much, but it kept her going. Kept her out of the Market . “What did you say?” she asked, clutching the pitcher to her chest . “I’m sorry, Lara. We’re sorry. But with Maggie getting bigger, it’s getting harder to spare the extra .” “Gary, please. Just…just think about it.” She tilted the heavy pitcher and leaned over to look past him. “Can I talk to Kate ?” “No, Lara. Maggie’s sleeping right now. Please, don’t make this any harder. We want to help you, we really do, but we have to consider our chil — ” Lara shoved past him. He stumbled aside, stammering as Lara entered the small living space . “Kate, please, don’t — ” Lara stilled. Kate, wide-eyed, held her arms over her rounded stomach, as though she could hide it . “Kate,” Lara breathed. They all knew the rule — one child per human couple . Gary swore, dragged the door back into place, and moved in front of Lara. He grasped her arms with strong, rough fingers, and bent down to eye level . “No one can know, Lara. No one !” he rasped . “I’d never tell anyone. But…how are you going to keep this secret ?” “We don’t know.” Kate stopped beside Gary, placing a hand on his shoulder . Gary swallowed, throat bobbing, and released Lara . “It’s getting harder to conceal now, and we aren’t earning as much with me having to stay hidden. Maggie doesn’t even know, but she’s been asking questions.” She grasped Gary’s hand. “We can’t let the gearheads find out, but we have nowhere else to go .” “We couldn’t tell anyone, not even you,” Gary said, his frown making the lines on his face harsher. “Kate and the baby need all they can get. We can’t spare anymore, not even in trade .” Lara studied Kate and knew he was right; she had dark circles under her eyes — in stark contrast to her pale skin — and her cheeks were hollow . “We’re sorry, Lara. We wish we could , but …” “I know,” Lara said, nodding. “I understand .” She was numb as Gary released Kate’s hand and opened the door. Two times in as many months, Lara’s world was falling apart. Passing through the doorway, she paused and turned to look at Gary and Kate. They’d helped Lara in her time of need, selflessly, and likely at great cost to their little family. It was more than anyone else would have done. More than they should have done . “Here.” Lara held the pitcher out to Gary . His eyebrows lifted. “No. We can’t accept that.” Behind him, Kate covered her mouth with her hand . “Please, just take it.” Her grip tightened on the handle. There were at least a dozen other people on the street who were likely to trade food for it, but who needed it more now than these two? “As a thank you. For all you’ve both done for me .” Kate’s eyes watered as Gary hesitantly accepted the offering. Lara ignored the pang of regret as she released it, busying her hands with replacing the cloth over her mouth and nose. “ Be safe .” “And you,” Kate said, voice thick . Lara walked away, feeling oddly heavy despite her empty hands . Her home stood on the northern edge of the human slums. Only the wide road — dirt and gravel, with long, rusted rails jutting from it at various points — and the wall separated her from the bot district, where the lights were already coming on. If they’d the means, she would’ve convinced Tabitha to move as far away from the wall as possible a long time ago . Lara’s shack was a mix of various materials, just like all the others. The base was brick, scavenged from a nearby building that collapsed when Lara was young. The wood boards that formed the walls had come from the same place. It was topped with a piece of sheet metal. She’d always enjoyed the pattering of rain and sand on the roof during storms . The chimes hanging near the entrance jingled in the wind. She’d used thin but durable lines — fishing line, Tabitha had said, though Lara didn’t know of any fish nearby — to hang a mismatched collection of spoons, forks, knives, and keys from a metal ring. The music they created was random, but there was nothing else like it in the settlement. She was surprised they hadn’t been stolen for scrap; it was likely because no one wanted to be in the shadow of the wall for long . She slid the door aside and entered, tossing her bag onto her pallet. She left a gap when she closed the door; a bit more airflow would help release some of the day’s heat . Removing her head wrap, she dropped it into a pile of spare cloth and moaned at the feel of cool air on her skin. She pulled the pin from her hair and let her braid fall free. Working her hair loose, she ran her fingers through it, closing her eyes as she massaged her scalp. Despite having been covered, dirt had worked its way in. If water weren’t so precious, she’d rinse her hair out . Inhaling deeply, she opened her eyes and lowered her hands . She walked across the small room, unfolded the sash at her waist, and removed the porcelain shard and her beat-up metal lighter. The latter was one of her most valuable items, one she refused to trade. Fuel wasn’t easy to come by, but having easy access to fire had saved Lara and Tabitha more than once during the frigid winters . Though the shadows inside were thickening, she found her lantern easily; it was always in the same place. After a few attempts, the lighter sparked to flame, and she lit the wick, filling the shack with
